questions.question: A film area reads density 2.0. What fraction of the illuminator's light passes through it, and roughly what illuminator luminance delivers the typical 30 cd/m2 to the interpreter's eye?
options: 10%; about 300 cd/m2
1%; about 3,000 cd/m2
questions.explanation: Density 2.0 transmits 10 to the minus 2 - one percent - of the incident light, so the illuminator must supply about 100 times the required viewing luminance: 30 x 100 = 3,000 cd/m2.

questions.question: After reading several routine films near density 2.0, an interpreter must evaluate a small region at density 3.9. The right tool and practice is:
options: The same strip illuminator at the same setting, viewed longer
A high-intensity spot viewer with variable intensity, with the surrounding film masked
Holding the film up to the room lights for diffuse illumination
A flashlight pressed against the back of the film
questions.explanation: Density 3.9 passes roughly one ten-thousandth of the light, demanding a high-intensity spot viewer; variable intensity and masking protect the interpreter's adaptation while the dense region is read.

questions.question: Which viewing-room condition is acceptable for film interpretation?
options: Shop-level lighting so the interpreter can fill out reports at the viewer
Total darkness with the illuminator at maximum for all films
Subdued background lighting positioned so nothing reflects off the film face and no bright source sits in the interpreter's view
A bright desk lamp aimed at the film to supplement the illuminator
questions.explanation: Background light must be subdued and arranged to avoid reflections off the film surface and bright sources in the field of view; both a bright room and total darkness degrade the eye's working adaptation.

questions.question: A double-film latitude technique uses a fast and a slow film read separately on an X-ray exposure. The density rule for each film is:
options: Each film must meet the full single-film minimum, typically 1.8, in the area it is used to interpret
Each film needs only 1.3 because two films were loaded
Only the films' combined density must exceed 2.6
The fast film has no density requirement
questions.explanation: The 1.3 per-film floor applies only to films viewed superimposed as a composite; any film read alone must meet the single-film minimum in the region it covers.

questions.question: Correct placement of a wire-type IQI on a weld radiograph is:
options: On adjacent base metal with the wires parallel to the weld
On the weld, with the wires perpendicular to the weld axis, source side unless inaccessible
On the cassette back beneath the weld
Anywhere on the film as long as the finest wire shows
questions.explanation: Wire IQIs lie directly on the weld with the wires crossing it perpendicular to its length so the wires sample the area of interest; source-side placement remains the default.

questions.question: Why must the shim under a hole-type IQI be larger than the IQI itself?
options: So the shim can hold the IQI flat against wind during exposure
So the IQI outline images clear of the shim edge on at least three sides, proving on the film that the IQI sat fully on shim material
To raise the film density under the IQI above 4.0
To make the IQI easier to recover after the exposure
questions.explanation: The visible margin around the plaque image is the film's own proof that the full IQI rested on reinforcement-compensating shim material rather than overhanging bare base metal.

questions.question: A typical near-vision requirement for personnel interpreting radiographs is:
options: Jaeger J-1 letters at not less than 12 inches with at least one eye, natural or corrected, verified at least annually
20/20 distance vision without correction, verified every five years
Reading the illuminator nameplate across the room
No formal requirement applies once Level II is reached
questions.explanation: Employer written practices based on documents such as SNT-TC-1A and NAS 410 typically require an annual documented near-vision check - J-1 at 12 inches or equivalent - with correction permitted, plus periodic color and gray-shade differentiation checks.

questions.question: The IQI body density on a gamma radiograph measures 3.0. A region of the weld reads 2.4. Under the typical minus 15% / plus 30% window, that region is:
options: Acceptable - 2.4 exceeds the gamma minimum of 2.0
Acceptable - the window only limits densities above the IQI reading
Outside the window, whose floor is 3.0 x 0.85 = 2.55 - so the region needs an additional IQI covering it or a reshoot
Outside the window, but interpretable since 2.4 is below 4.0
questions.explanation: The allowed band is 2.55 to 3.90; at 2.4 the region is lighter than the demonstrated sensitivity represents, and the remedy is procedural - another IQI for that area or another exposure - not a judgment call.

questions.question: Jagged, branched black lines resembling lightning on a radiograph are characteristic of:
options: Stress cracking in the weld
Static discharge from sliding films apart in dry conditions
Backscatter through the cassette
Reticulation from processing temperature shock
questions.explanation: Static marks print as branched, lightning-like black lines or dot clusters caused by discharge when films are slid apart or unwrapped briskly in dry air - a handling artifact, not a part condition.

questions.question: Why does a girth-weld crew punch a zero reference point on the pipe and record a direction convention for the numbered marker band?
options: So the film density can be corrected for wall thickness
So every marker image on every film maps to one unique physical location on the weld, allowing a repair crew to find the exact spot years later
Because lead numbers cannot be exposed without a zero present
To indicate which side of the wall the IQI was placed on
questions.explanation: The permanent zero reference and stated direction make the marker numbering unambiguous, so a film calling out a flaw at a given station leads back to one - and only one - point on the actual part.
